dprs.title: 8KB Fixed Page Size
dprs.decision: PostgreSQL uses a fixed 8KB page size (BLCKSZ) as the fundamental unit of storage and I/O.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: 8KB aligns with filesystem blocks
INFERRED: Most rows fit in 8KB
INFERRED: OS cache works well with 8KB
rejected_alternatives: Variable page sizes - complexity in buffer management
dprs.durability_reasoning: Changing requires recompilation and full database rebuild
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: Modern NVMe SSDs use 16-32KB internal pages
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Every storage operation depends on this constant
causal_dependencies: DPR-002: TOAST exists because of 8KB limit

dprs.title: TOAST - The Oversized-Attribute Storage Technique
dprs.decision: Values >2KB automatically compressed/moved to separate TOAST tables.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Large values accessed less frequently
INFERRED: Compression ratios good enough
INFERRED: Decompression overhead acceptable
rejected_alternatives: Fail on large values - users need large data
dprs.durability_reasoning: Deeply integrated into tuple storage and MVCC
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: Design is stable, though modern compression could improve
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Affects query planning, storage, backup, replication
causal_dependencies: DPR-001: Exists because 8KB pages too small
DPR-003: MVCC must handle TOAST pointers
DPR-006: Vacuum must clean TOAST tables

dprs.title: MVCC via Tuple Versioning in Heap
dprs.decision: MVCC by storing multiple row versions in heap with xmin/xmax for visibility.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Transactions are short-lived
INFERRED: Reads tolerate dead tuples
INFERRED: Vacuum keeps up
rejected_alternatives: Single version + undo - old version access expensive
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: High-update workloads create massive bloat, long transactions block vacuum
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Every read/write depends on MVCC visibility rules

decay_alert.decay_evidence: High-update workloads on cloud storage creating bloat faster than vacuum can clean
decay_alert.recommended_monitor_query: SELECT schemaname, tablename, n_dead_tup, n_live_tup, round(100.0 * n_dead_tup / NULLIF(n_live_tup + n_dead_tup, 0), 2) as dead_pct FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE n_dead_tup > 10000 ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC

dprs.title: 32-bit Transaction ID with Wraparound
dprs.decision: XIDs are 32-bit wrapping after ~4B transactions, requiring periodic vacuum freeze.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Won't exceed 2B between freezes
INFERRED: Vacuum freeze completes in time
INFERRED: 32-bit saves enough space
dprs.durability_reasoning: XID size baked into tuple header format
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: High-throughput systems hit limits quickly, large tables take hours to freeze
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Can cause database shutdown and data loss

decay_alert.decay_evidence: High-throughput OLTP systems (100K+ TPS) can generate 2B transactions in ~6 hours
decay_alert.recommended_monitor_query: SELECT datname, age(datfrozenxid) as xid_age, 2000000000 - age(datfrozenxid) as xids_until_wraparound FROM pg_database WHERE datallowconn ORDER BY age(datfrozenxid) DESC

dprs.title: HOT (Heap-Only Tuple) Updates
dprs.decision: UPDATEs not changing indexed columns and fitting on same page create HOT chains without index updates.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Most updates don't change indexed columns
INFERRED: Pages have space
INFERRED: Chain traversal < index update cost
dprs.durability_reasoning: Critical performance optimization for update-heavy workloads
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: Many indexed columns or large rows may not benefit
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Affects update performance, index maintenance, vacuum

dprs.title: Vacuum as Separate Maintenance Process
dprs.decision: Dead tuple cleanup via separate VACUUM process, not inline.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Can run frequently enough
INFERRED: Maintenance windows exist
INFERRED: Vacuum I/O manageable
INFERRED: Tables small enough
rejected_alternatives: Undo log auto-cleanup - undo storage complexity
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: TB+ tables take hours, high update rates outpace vacuum, cloud I/O costs
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Affects bloat, performance, wraparound safety, storage costs

decay_alert.decay_evidence: TB-scale tables taking 6-12 hours to vacuum, cloud storage IOPS limits making vacuum expensive
decay_alert.recommended_monitor_query: SELECT pid, datname, relid::regclass, phase, heap_blks_total, heap_blks_scanned, round(100.0 * heap_blks_scanned / NULLIF(heap_blks_total, 0), 2) as pct_complete FROM pg_stat_progress_vacuum
decay_alert.live_reasoning:
The original decay evidence (TB-scale tables taking 6-12 hours to vacuum, cloud storage IOPS limits making vacuum expensive) directly contradicts the inferred assumptions that vacuum can run frequently enough, maintenance windows exist, vacuum I/O is manageable, and tables are small enough. These issues represent fundamental challenges of scale and cost.

The recent commit 'Refactor autovacuum threshold calculations' by Tom Lane addresses the *scheduling* or *triggering* logic for autovacuum, an *automatic* background process. While autovacuum is related to vacuuming, this commit does not address the core problems identified by the decay evidence: the *duration* and *cost* of vacuuming very large tables. Improving when autovacuum runs does not make the actual vacuum operation on TB-scale tables faster or cheaper, nor does it create more maintenance windows or reduce IOPS costs for a 'separate maintenance process'. Therefore, the assumption that a 'separate maintenance process' for vacuuming is viable under these conditions still does not hold.

The commit is considered 'neutral' because it doesn't directly strengthen or weaken the feasibility of the *separate maintenance process* itself. It's an optimization for the automatic counterpart, which might indirectly alleviate some pressure but doesn't resolve the fundamental issues of scale and cost for a dedicated, separate vacuum operation. No new decay signal is present; the commit is an improvement/refactoring, not an indication of a new problem.

dprs.title: Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) for Durability
dprs.decision: All modifications written to sequential WAL before data pages.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Sequential WAL >> random data writes
INFERRED: fsync actually persists
INFERRED: WAL volume manageable
rejected_alternatives: Synchronous data writes - random I/O too slow
dprs.durability_reasoning: Foundation of durability and crash recovery
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: NVMe blurs sequential vs random I/O, cloud storage different guarantees
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Every write generates WAL, enables replication/backup/recovery
causal_dependencies: DPR-013: Full-page writes for torn pages

decay_alert.live_reasoning: The commit 'Improve WAL segment recycling under heavy write load' directly addresses the inferred assumption 'WAL volume manageable'. By improving recycling efficiency, especially under heavy load, it makes the management of WAL volume more robust and efficient. This strengthens the assumption that WAL volume can be effectively managed, preventing it from growing unbounded or causing performance issues due to excessive disk usage. It does not challenge the other inferred assumptions regarding sequential writes or fsync reliability. The commit is a positive improvement, not an indicator of decay.

dprs.title: Autovacuum as Background Daemon
dprs.decision: Background daemon auto-schedules VACUUM/ANALYZE based on table activity.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: 20% threshold appropriate
INFERRED: 3 workers sufficient
INFERRED: Cost throttling prevents I/O overwhelm
dprs.durability_reasoning: Critical for preventing bloat and wraparound
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: 20% of 1TB = 200GB too much, high-velocity overwhelms workers
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Misconfiguration is top cause of production issues

decay_alert.decay_evidence: 20% of 1TB table = 200GB of changes before vacuum triggers, causing massive bloat
decay_alert.recommended_monitor_query: SELECT schemaname, tablename, n_tup_ins + n_tup_upd + n_tup_del as total_changes, n_live_tup, round(100.0 * (n_tup_ins + n_tup_upd + n_tup_del) / NULLIF(n_live_tup, 0), 2) as change_pct FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE pg_total_relation_size(schemaname||'.'||tablename) > 10737418240 ORDER BY total_changes DESC
decay_alert.live_reasoning: The original decay evidence already highlighted that the '20% threshold appropriate' part of the assumption was problematic, leading to massive bloat (200GB for a 1TB table). The recent commit, 'Refactor autovacuum threshold calculations,' directly addresses this specific component. The act of refactoring implies that the previous calculations, and thus the underlying assumption about the 20% threshold's appropriateness, were found to be suboptimal or incorrect. Therefore, this part of the assumption no longer holds. The commit weakens the overall assumption by confirming the need for significant changes to address its flaws. While the commit doesn't directly address the '3 workers sufficient' or 'cost throttling' parts, the most critical and previously decayed part of the assumption is being actively changed, significantly lowering confidence in the assumption as a whole.
decay_alert.new_decay_signal: The necessity of refactoring autovacuum threshold calculations confirms that the original assumption regarding the 20% threshold's appropriateness was flawed.

dprs.title: Multi-Process Architecture
dprs.decision: Process-per-connection, not multi-threaded.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Process overhead acceptable
INFERRED: External pooling (pgBouncer)
INFERRED: Process isolation = stability
rejected_alternatives: Event loop - can't use multiple cores
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: High connection counts consume excessive memory, cloud charges for memory
causal_dependencies: DPR-011: Shared memory for buffer pool

decay_alert.live_reasoning: The commit 'Improve parallel query worker allocation' indicates active development and optimization *within* the multi-process architecture. This suggests that the multi-process model is still the chosen paradigm and is being refined for better performance and efficiency. This directly strengthens the assumption that process overhead is acceptable (or being made more acceptable through optimization) and implicitly supports the continued value of process isolation for stability. It does not introduce any signal that the architecture is failing or becoming problematic; rather, it shows continued investment.

dprs.title: Snapshot Isolation as Default
dprs.decision: READ COMMITTED and REPEATABLE READ snapshot isolation via MVCC.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Apps tolerate non-repeatable reads
INFERRED: Write skew rare
INFERRED: Apps use explicit locking when needed
explicit_constraints: READ COMMITTED: new snapshot per statement
REPEATABLE READ: snapshot at transaction start

dprs.title: Shared Buffer Pool with Clock-Sweep
dprs.decision: Shared buffer pool with clock-sweep (LRU approximation) eviction.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Clock-sweep â LRU
INFERRED: 25-40% RAM for shared_buffers
INFERRED: OS cache helps
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: Large working sets may not fit, cloud RAM expensive, NVMe reduces cache miss cost
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Every read/write goes through buffer pool

decay_alert.decay_evidence: Cloud instances with limited RAM make large buffer pools expensive, NVMe reducing cache miss penalty
decay_alert.recommended_monitor_query: SELECT sum(heap_blks_read) as heap_read, sum(heap_blks_hit) as heap_hit, round(100.0 * sum(heap_blks_hit) / NULLIF(sum(heap_blks_hit) + sum(heap_blks_read), 0), 2) as cache_hit_ratio FROM pg_statio_user_tables
decay_alert.live_reasoning: The original decay evidence pointed to challenges with large `shared_buffers` (cost in cloud, reduced NVMe penalty). However, the recent commit 'Optimize buffer pool clock-sweep for large shared_buffers' directly addresses the performance aspect of large buffer pools. This indicates continued investment in making large `shared_buffers` performant and viable, which strengthens the underlying assumption that large `shared_buffers` are a desirable configuration and that clock-sweep is an effective mechanism for managing them. While the cost aspect in cloud instances remains, the performance optimization makes the investment in larger `shared_buffers` more justifiable, thereby reinforcing the assumption.

dprs.title: Checkpoint-Based Crash Recovery
dprs.decision: Periodic checkpoints write all dirty buffers, allowing recovery from last checkpoint.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Checkpoint I/O completes in time
INFERRED: Recovery time acceptable
INFERRED: Checkpoint I/O manageable
explicit_constraints: Triggered by time (5min) or WAL volume
rejected_alternatives: Continuous writing - recovery replays all WAL
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: Large shared_buffers make checkpoints I/O intensive, cloud IOPS limits
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Affects recovery time, I/O patterns, WAL volume

decay_alert.live_reasoning: The assumption concerns the performance and manageability of checkpoint I/O and acceptable recovery time. The recent commit 'Improve WAL segment recycling under heavy write load' focuses on optimizing Write-Ahead Log (WAL) management. While not directly about checkpoint I/O, WAL is a fundamental component of crash recovery, and checkpoints are used to reduce the amount of WAL that needs to be replayed. Efficient WAL segment recycling, especially under heavy write load, indicates that the system is better at managing its I/O and disk resources in high-stress scenarios. This improvement in a closely related I/O-intensive subsystem indirectly reduces potential pressure points that could impact checkpoint performance or manageability. By making the system more robust under heavy write load, it strengthens the overall confidence that critical I/O operations, including checkpoints, remain manageable and performant. No new decay signal is present; the commit represents an optimization.

dprs.title: Full-Page Writes for Torn Page Protection
dprs.decision: First modification after checkpoint writes entire 8KB page to WAL.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Storage doesn't guarantee atomic 8KB
INFERRED: Torn pages rare but catastrophic
INFERRED: WAL increase acceptable
rejected_alternatives: Checksums only - detect but don't fix
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: Storage still doesn't guarantee atomic 8KB writes
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Significantly increases WAL volume (2-3x after checkpoints)

dprs.title: Streaming Replication via WAL Shipping
dprs.decision: Replication by streaming WAL from primary to standby for replay.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Network bandwidth sufficient
INFERRED: Standby can keep up
INFERRED: WAL contains all needed info

dprs.title: External Connection Pooling Required
dprs.decision: Connection pooling handled externally (pgBouncer, pgPool) rather than in-server.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: External pooling acceptable
INFERRED: Pooler won't be bottleneck
explicit_constraints: External tool required for high connection counts
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Required for production deployments with many connections

dprs.title: Cost-Based Query Optimizer
dprs.decision: Use cost-based optimization with selectivity estimates from pg_statistic to choose query plans
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Statistics are accurate enough for cost estimation
INFERRED: Cost model reflects actual hardware performance

dprs.title: Extensible Type System
dprs.decision: Allow user-defined types, operators, and functions registered in system catalogs
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Type system extensibility won't create security risks
INFERRED: Custom types perform comparably to built-in types

dprs.title: B-tree as Default Index
dprs.decision: Use B-tree as the default and most optimized index access method
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Most queries use equality and range predicates
INFERRED: B-tree overhead is acceptable for all table sizes

dprs.title: Logical Replication via Publish/Subscribe
dprs.decision: Implement logical replication using publication/subscription model decoding WAL
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: WAL contains sufficient info for logical decoding
INFERRED: Subscription lag is acceptable for most use cases

dprs.title: Table Partitioning via Inheritance
dprs.decision: Implement declarative partitioning built on table inheritance with partition pruning
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Partition count stays manageable (< 1000)
INFERRED: Partition key aligns with query patterns

dprs.title: Parallel Query Execution
dprs.decision: Support parallel query execution using background workers for seq scan, hash join, aggregation
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Multi-core CPUs are standard
INFERRED: Parallel overhead is justified for large scans

dprs.title: JIT Compilation via LLVM
dprs.decision: Integrate LLVM-based JIT compilation for expression evaluation and tuple deforming
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: LLVM API remains stable across versions
INFERRED: JIT compilation time is amortized over query execution

dprs.title: Asynchronous I/O (AIO) Subsystem
dprs.decision: Introduce native async I/O infrastructure replacing synchronous read/write paths
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: io_uring is mature enough for production databases
INFERRED: Async patterns won't introduce subtle concurrency bugs

dprs.title: 64-bit Transaction IDs
dprs.decision: Migrate from 32-bit to 64-bit transaction IDs to eliminate wraparound risk
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Migration path exists without breaking pg_upgrade
INFERRED: 64-bit XID overhead is negligible on modern hardware

dprs.title: Incremental Backup Support
dprs.decision: Add native incremental backup via WAL summarization tracking changed blocks
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: WAL summarization overhead is minimal
INFERRED: Block-level tracking is sufficient for backup granularity

dprs.title: JSON_TABLE SQL Standard Support
dprs.decision: Implement SQL/JSON JSON_TABLE for structured extraction from JSON documents
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: JSON workloads are significant enough to warrant SQL-standard support
INFERRED: jsonpath execution is performant for large documents

dprs.title: Direct I/O Support
dprs.decision: Add O_DIRECT support for bypassing OS page cache when PostgreSQL manages its own buffer pool
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: PostgreSQL buffer management is superior to OS cache for DB workloads
INFERRED: All storage devices support O_DIRECT efficiently

dprs.title: Statistics Import/Export
dprs.decision: Allow importing/exporting planner statistics for reproducible query plans across environments
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Statistics format remains stable across versions
INFERRED: Exported statistics are meaningful across different data distributions

dprs.title: Virtual WAL for Logical Replication
dprs.decision: Decouple logical replication from physical WAL format for cross-version compatibility
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Logical decoding abstraction doesn't lose critical change information
INFERRED: Performance overhead of abstraction layer is acceptable

dprs.title: Self-Tuning Shared Buffers
dprs.decision: Research adaptive shared buffer sizing based on workload pressure instead of static configuration
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Workload patterns are predictable enough for auto-tuning
INFERRED: Dynamic resizing won't cause performance regressions under edge cases

ctg_edges.explanation: 8KB page size forces TOAST for large values
Vacuum assumes manageable dead tuple rate
HOT updates must fit within 8KB page
Checkpoints write dirty buffers from pool
Optimizer triggers JIT for expensive expressions
Extensible types allow custom index operators
WAL enables logical decoding for replication
Physical WAL dependency drives virtual WAL need
Partition pruning relies on optimizer cost estimates
MVCC visibility depends on XID width
64-bit XIDs may change autovacuum freeze logic
AIO changes buffer pool I/O patterns
AIO subsystem enables efficient direct I/O
Buffer pool must be sufficient when bypassing OS cache
Static buffers drive need for self-tuning
Extensible types enable JSON path operations
Parallel workers benefit from async I/O
Process model limits parallel worker count
Streaming replication infrastructure enables logical replication
JIT must handle async execution contexts
MVCC dead tuples consume buffer space
Direct I/O changes full-page write behavior

decay_alerts.title: MVCC via Tuple Versioning in Heap
decay_alerts.decay_evidence: High-update workloads on cloud storage creating bloat faster than vacuum can clean
decay_alerts.recommended_monitor_query: SELECT schemaname, tablename, n_dead_tup, n_live_tup, round(100.0 * n_dead_tup / NULLIF(n_live_tup + n_dead_tup, 0), 2) as dead_pct FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE n_dead_tup > 10000 ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC

decay_alerts.title: 32-bit Transaction ID with Wraparound
decay_alerts.decay_evidence: High-throughput OLTP systems (100K+ TPS) can generate 2B transactions in ~6 hours
decay_alerts.recommended_monitor_query: SELECT datname, age(datfrozenxid) as xid_age, 2000000000 - age(datfrozenxid) as xids_until_wraparound FROM pg_database WHERE datallowconn ORDER BY age(datfrozenxid) DESC

decay_alerts.title: Vacuum as Separate Maintenance Process
decay_alerts.decay_evidence: TB-scale tables taking 6-12 hours to vacuum, cloud storage IOPS limits making vacuum expensive
decay_alerts.recommended_monitor_query: SELECT pid, datname, relid::regclass, phase, heap_blks_total, heap_blks_scanned, round(100.0 * heap_blks_scanned / NULLIF(heap_blks_total, 0), 2) as pct_complete FROM pg_stat_progress_vacuum

decay_alerts.title: Autovacuum as Background Daemon
decay_alerts.decay_evidence: 20% of 1TB table = 200GB of changes before vacuum triggers, causing massive bloat
decay_alerts.recommended_monitor_query: SELECT schemaname, tablename, n_tup_ins + n_tup_upd + n_tup_del as total_changes, n_live_tup, round(100.0 * (n_tup_ins + n_tup_upd + n_tup_del) / NULLIF(n_live_tup, 0), 2) as change_pct FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE pg_total_relation_size(schemaname||'.'||tablename) > 10737418240 ORDER BY total_changes DESC

decay_alerts.title: Shared Buffer Pool with Clock-Sweep
decay_alerts.decay_evidence: Cloud instances with limited RAM make large buffer pools expensive, NVMe reducing cache miss penalty
decay_alerts.recommended_monitor_query: SELECT sum(heap_blks_read) as heap_read, sum(heap_blks_hit) as heap_hit, round(100.0 * sum(heap_blks_hit) / NULLIF(sum(heap_blks_hit) + sum(heap_blks_read), 0), 2) as cache_hit_ratio FROM pg_statio_user_tables
